topics.topic_title: THE BEGINNING AND SPREAD OF IRON WORKING AND FARMING

sub_topics.title: Development of Iron-Technology and Farming
competences: Analyse impact of Iron technology in farming

instructional_blocks.hook: Imagine how life would be without farming and iron tools! How did farming start? Were iron tools always there since the beginning of human life?
teacher_steps: Ask learners prior to the lesson to research on early areas of farming and iron working in the Middle East and Africa
Ask learners to present their findings
Display a map showing early areas of farming and iron working in the Middle East and Africa
Ask learners to draw the map
Consolidates using the map
learner_steps: Research on early areas of farming and iron working in the Middle East and Africa

teacher_steps: Brainstorming: Ask learners to mention settlement areas of Iron Age farmers in Zambia
Display a map showing settlement areas of Iron Age farmers in Zambia
Ask learners to identify settlement areas of Iron Age farmers in Zambia
Consolidate key points for the learners
Instruct learners to draw the map
learner_steps: Mention settlement areas of Iron Age farmers in Zambia

teacher_steps: Display a chart showing trade at Ing'ombe Ilede
Ask learners to study the chart
Conduct a class discussion based on the chart
Ask learners to draw the diagram on the chart
Ask learners to refer to the chart to complete the table
learner_steps: Study the chart showing trade at Ing'ombe Ilede
Complete the table on items found, imported and exported at Ing'ombe Ilede

teacher_steps: Ask learners to mention the Iron Age tools used in agriculture, fishing, hunting and trading
Consolidate learners' responses using pictures
Divide learners in groups
Ask learners in groups to analyse the importance of iron technology in agriculture, fishing, hunting, trading and construction
Ask group representatives to make presentations to the class
Write the main points on the board
learner_steps: Mention Iron Age tools used in various activities
Work in groups to analyse importance of iron technology

sub_topics.short_notes:
Early Areas of Farming and Iron working in the Middle East: Farming began around 10000 BC in the Middle East between rivers Tigris and Euphrates known as Fertile Crescent or Mesopotamia. Iron working was started by the Hittites of Asia Minor (Turkey) who began the use of iron to make tools around 3000 B.C.

Early Areas of Farming and Iron working in Africa: From the Middle East, knowledge of farming and iron working spread to Egypt along the Nile Valley around 650 B.C. In Africa, iron working began along the Nile Valley in Egypt and spread to Sudan (Kush) where an iron working centre was established at Meroe. The knowledge of iron working then spread further to Ethiopia (Axum).

Settlement areas of Iron Age farmers in Zambia: Kalundu and Isamu Pati in Kalomo District; Ing'ombe Ilede in Siavonga District.

Relating Ing'ombe Ilede to the Global Trade Environment: Ing'ombe Ilede was an Early Iron Age trading centre located near the confluence of Zambezi and Lusitu rivers in Siavonga District, Southern Province. The people of Ing'ombe Ilede possessed salt and ivory. They imported gold and ivory from the south as far as Zimbabwe. Copper, slaves, ivory and iron objects were imported from the north as far as Katanga. These items attracted foreign traders from Asia and the Middle East. These traders came to Ing'ombe Ilede from the east coast using the Zambezi River. They brought iron objects, beads, sea shells and cloth. This led to the integration of Ing'ombe Ilede into the international trade environment. Similarly, modern Zambia has natural resources which enables it to be involved in global trade. Zambia exports goods such as minerals, maize and electricity to other countries. It also imports goods like oil, machinery, foodstuffs, and other goods.

The importance of Iron technology: Agriculture - Iron technology led to increased food production. Iron tools like hoes, axes and picks enabled early farmers to cultivate large portions of land. Increased Population - Increased food production led to population increase. Fishing - Iron technology led to increased improved fishing using iron tools such as spears and hooks. Hunting - Iron technology improved hunting through use of spears and arrows made of iron. In modern times guns made of iron components are used to hunt animals by those with licences. Trading - Black smith make iron tools such as hoes, axes, slashers, cutters for sale on the market. Metal fabrication industries also make modern iron equipment and tools for sale for revenue generation. Construction - Iron is used in roofing houses, making window frames, building poles and others to establishment permanent infrastructure. Migration - Iron tools encouraged people to migrate into new areas of settlement.

vocabulary.definition: refers to crop production and rearing of livestock
refers growing of crops and rearing of animals
refers to an area between rivers Tigris and Euphrates in the Middle East where farming began in the world
Domesticated animals like goats, pigs, chickens, cattle and many more
refers to a mineral extracted from rocks used to farming tools
refers to the exchange of goods for goods among the various groups of people in the past

questions: What challenges would early and modern farmers have faced if Iron-Technology had not been discovered?
You have observed a reduction in food production in your community. Using the knowledge of iron technology and farming, how would you promote crop production in your local community?
Many young people in Zambia today have remained jobless even after acquiring tertiary education. Explain how young people can utilise iron technology to earn a living.

sub_topics.title: Early Bantu settlement in Africa
competences: Locate origins of the Bantu Speaking people of Africa

instructional_blocks.hook: What language is the word 'Bantu?' Some people say that the Bantu are only found in Zambia.
teacher_steps: Divide learners in groups
Distribute flash cards bearing the word 'person'
Instruct learners to discuss and write the word 'person' in different languages
Ask learners to analyse similarities in the words
Ask group representatives to present their findings to the class
Write and consolidate the points from the learners
learner_steps: Write the word 'person' in different languages

teacher_steps: Prior to this lesson, ask learners to carry out a research about the Bantu speaking people
Divide learners into groups
Distribute maps showing groups of people found in Africa
Ask learners to identify the Bantu speaking people from the maps
Instruct group representatives to report their findings to the class
Write the points on the board
learner_steps: Carry out research about Bantu speaking people
Study maps showing groups of people in Africa
Identify Bantu speaking people from maps

teacher_steps: Divide learners into small groups
Distribute blank maps of Africa
Ask each group to locate the early Bantu settlement in West Africa on the blank map provided
Instruct learners to mark the area settled by Bantu-speaking people in West Africa
Ask groups to share their completed maps with the class
Display the map showing the Benue Valley in West Africa
learner_steps: Locate early Bantu settlement in West Africa
Mark the Benue Region on the map

teacher_steps: Ask learners to work in groups
Distribute documents explaining reasons for the Bantu migrations from West Africa
Ask learners to study the document
Tell learners to discuss the reasons for the Bantu migrations from West Africa
Ask group representatives to report their findings to the class
Write the main points on the board
Instruct the learners to copy the points in their books
learner_steps: Discuss reasons for Bantu migrations from West Africa

teacher_steps: Display a map on the course of the Bantu migrations from West Africa
Ask learners to study the map
Guide learners to discuss the course of the Bantu migrations from West Africa
Summarise the main points
learner_steps: Discuss the course of Bantu migrations from West Africa

sub_topics.short_notes:
The term Bantu: The Bantu are people who speak similar languages. For example, the word 'person' has the root 'ntu' and the prefix 'mu' in various Bantu languages. The term Bantu refers to a large family of languages spoken across central Eastern and Southern Africa.

Identifying the Bantu Speaking People: There are many groups of Bantu speaking people in Africa. For example, Luba, Lunda, Zulu, Ndebele, Sukuma, Shona, Yao, Kikuyu and many others.

Early Bantu settlement in West Africa: The early Bantu settled in West Africa in the Benue Region located between Lake Chad and the Cameroon Highlands.

Reasons for the Bantu migrations from West Africa: Population increase; Search for fertile land; Drought and climate change; Conflicts and wars; Diseases; Love for adventure and exploration; Iron working technology; Expansion of trade; Witchcraft; Slave trade; Succession disputes; Family quarrels.

The course of the Bantu migrations from West Africa: The Central route - movements from West Africa into the Congo Basin forming the Luba and Lunda kingdoms. The Eastern route - movements from West Africa into the Great Lakes region such the Baganda, Banyoro, Basoga, and others proceeded southwards such as Tonga, Shona and Nguni groups. The Western route - movement from West Africa into Southern Africa such as Ovambo, Okavango, Herero. Some settled in Angola, Namibia.

vocabulary.definition: refers to a group of Africans found south of the Sahara who have similar languages and culture
the movement of people from one place to another in search of food or better living conditions
the region in West Africa where the Bantu Speaking people are believed to have originated from
the area of Africa located south of the Saharan desert where the Bantu people migrated and settled
the act of establishing communities in new areas

sub_topics.title: Bantu Migrations into Zambia
competences: Use knowledge of common origins of Zambian people to promote national unity

teacher_steps: Ask learners to draw table showing causes for migrations in modern day Zambia and complete it
Display a chart showing causes of the Bantu migrations into Zambia
Ask learners to classify the causes into political, economic and social categories as shown in the table
Conduct a whole class discussion to consolidate the causes of the Bantu migrations
Instruct learners to write the consolidated points
learner_steps: Draw table showing causes for migrations in modern day Zambia
Study chart showing causes of Bantu migrations into Zambia
Classify causes into political, economic and social categories

teacher_steps: Display the map showing the course of Bantu migrations into Zambia
Ask learners to draw the map
Instruct learners to study the map to trace migration routes of the Bantu speaking groups into Zambia
Whole class discussion on the course of the Bantu migrations into Zambia
learner_steps: Trace migration routes on the map

teacher_steps: Display the map showing historical settlements for Bantu speaking people
Ask learners to draw the map in their books
Consolidates the information on the map
learner_steps: Draw map showing historical settlements of Bantu speaking people in Zambia

teacher_steps: Ask learners prior to this lesson to find out if the Bantu migrations have contributed to national unity
Divide learners into two groups to debate the motion: Bantu migrations brought national unity in Zambia
Summarise the debate by analysing the effects of Bantu migrations on national unity. Teacher must emphasise that the Bantu migrations have contributed to national unity
learner_steps: Research on whether Bantu migrations contributed to national unity
Note summary points on effects of Bantu migrations on national unity

teacher_steps: Circulate pictures depicting shared heritage
Ask learners to study the pictures
Ask learners to analyse how the pictures depict the use of shared heritage to foster national unity
Whole class discussion
Consolidates and writes the main points
learner_steps: Analyse how pictures show use of shared heritage to foster national unity

sub_topics.short_notes:
Causes for the Bantu migrations into Zambia - From Katanga: Conflict over land; Expansion of powerful kingdoms; Internal conflicts and wars; Succession disputes; Shortage of water resources; Desire to receive tribute and control trade; Discovery and use of iron technology; Need for fertile land; Shortage of pasture for animals; Social causes; Adventure; Family quarrels; Overpopulation in their original homeland; Witchcraft. From South Africa - Effects of Mfecane: The wars of Shaka led to a wave of wars and forced migrations across southern Africa. Many groups like the Ngoni, Ndebele and the Kololo migrated to escape Zulu military expansion.

The course of the Bantu migrations into Zambia: Movement from the Great Lakes Region - The Bantu who migrated in to this group include the Tonga, Lungu and Tabwa. They moved from East Africa and settled Zambia. Movement from Katanga - From Luba include: Bemba, Chewa, Soli, Lenje, Lala, Lamba, Tumbuka and many more. From Lunda include: Lozi, Lunda, Luvale, Chokwe, Mbunda, Kaonde and many. Movement from South Africa - This included the Ngoni and Kololo.

The Historical settlement for Bantu speaking people in Zambia: Central province- Lala, Lenje, Swaka, Sala and many more; Eastern province – Chewa, Chikunda, Nsenga, Ngoni, Senga, Tumbuka and many more; Luapula province –Lunda, Kabende, Ushi, Chishinga and many more; Muchinga province – Bemba, Bisa, Lambia, Namwanga and many more; Northern province – Bemba, Mambwe, Lungu; North-western – Lunda, Luvale, Chokwe, Kaonde, Luchazi, Mbunda, Ndembu and many more; Southern province – Tonga, Ila, Toka-leya, Ila and many more; Western province – Lozi, Nkoya, Subiya, Nyengo, Kwangwa, Mbunda and many more; Lusaka province- Soli, Lenje; Copperbelt province- Lamba.

Effects of the Bantu migrations on national unity: Shared heritage – common identity promotes national unity. Cultural exchange – as different communities settled they shared customs, traditions and values that fostered national unity. Diverse ethnic groups share same historical experiences based on common origins. This is a unifying factor. Pre-colonial political structures provided early models of leadership and community cohesion that later influenced modern state-building. Intermarriages and social bonds help break ethnic barriers leading to stronger social bonds that contribute to national unity. The need to coexist in a shared territory prompts new social norms and practices. Migrating groups brought with them new innovations in agriculture, iron working and trade which contributed to a shared economy.

Using shared heritage to foster national unity: Shared heritage refers to common cultural, historical and social elements that different ethnic groups in Zambia have. Examples of shared heritage in Zambia include ceremonies, traditional attire, food, songs, shrines and others. They help to foster appreciation and understanding of each other's culture. Zambian societies emphasise values such as respect, hospitality and communal living which are shared across different ethnic groups. Proverbs and folk tales from different tribes often carry similar messages about unity and cooperation. Language as a unifying factor: some local languages such as Bemba, Nyanja Tonga and Lozi are widely spoken across different regions. Intermarriages and social integration. Shared heritage fosters national unity by promoting mutual respect, encouraging patriotism, building peace, social cohesion and strengthening national pride.

vocabulary.definition: a chief who has overall control over sub-chiefs
a show of loyalty to the traditional leaders through giving items and labour

questions: In most of the Pre-colonial Centralised societies of Zambia, the Chief controlled the Army. Why is it an important aspect of every society including modern Zambia to have an army centrally controlled?

Most Pre-colonial societies in Zambia were located in specific regions of the country. However, people have now changed and different ethnic groups are now settling in any part of the country. What factors have contributed to this change in settlement patterns?
How can historical awareness contribute to fostering a sense of understanding, respect and unity among various ethnic and cultural groups within Zambia?

sub_topics.title: Decentralised societies in Pre-colonial Zambia

teacher_steps: Display chart showing decentralised societies in Zambia
Ask learners to study the dsplayed chart
Consolidates the points on decentralised societies
Ask learners to write the list in their books
Display the map
Ask learners to draw the map of Zambia and indicate the location of decentralised societies
Consolidate the points
learner_steps: Draw map of Zambia showing location of decentralised societies

teacher_steps: Divide the class into groups
Distribute the documents containing information on features of decentralised societies
Ask learners to study the documents
Instruct learners to discuss and write the features of decentralised societies
Ask group representatives to present their findings to the class
learner_steps: Study documents on features of decentralised societies
Discuss and write features of decentralised societies

sub_topics.short_notes:
Decentralised societies in pre-colonial Zambia were communities or ethnic groups that did not have a centralised form of government. These societies were organised around clans, lineages, or family groups, with leadership distributed across elders or village heads. These were societies that did not have a strong central government and were not ruled by kings or chiefs. Examples of decentralised societies in Zambia include the Tonga, Ila, Lenje, Sala, Soli and others.

Areas of settlement for decentralised societies in Zambia: Southern province – Tonga, Ila; Central province – Lenje, Sala; Lusaka province - Soli.

Key features of decentralised societies in Zambia: Loose political structure – they did not have chiefs and kings, leadership was distributed among village elders, clan leaders or family heads. They were mainly farmers, who grew crops such as millet and sorghum and kept livestock such as cattle goats, sheep and pigs. They also reared chickens. They were involved in local trade. They did not have organised military structure and this exposed them to attack from powerful groups. Societies were organised around extended families with strong kinship ties. Ceremonies, traditions and oral history played a role in maintaining cultural identity and governance.

sub_topics.title: Centralised Societies
competences: Integrate features of centralised societies in modern Zambian society

teacher_steps: Display chart showing centralised societies in Zambia
Ask learners to study the chart
Consolidates the points on centralised societies
Ask learners to write the points in their books
Display the map
Ask learners to draw the map of Zambia and indicate the location of centralised societies
Consolidate the points
learner_steps: Draw map of Zambia showing location of centralised societies

teacher_steps: Distribute the documents containing information on features of centralised societies
Ask learners to study the documents
Instruct learners to discuss and write the features of centralised societies
learner_steps: Study documents on features of centralised societies

teacher_steps: Divide learners into groups
Ask learners to draw the table
Ask learners to complete the table by showing how features of centralised societies have been integrated in the modern Zambian society
Ask learners to present their work to the class
Display a complete chart showing how features of centralised societies have been integrated in the modern Zambian society
Ask learners to copy the completed table in their books
learner_steps: Complete table showing how features of centralised societies have been integrated in modern Zambian society

sub_topics.short_notes:
Centralised societies are societies that have a strong central government and are led either by a chief, king or queen. Examples of centralised societies in pre-colonial Zambia include the Bemba, Chewa, Kaonde, Lunda, Luyi, Luvale and Ngoni.

Centralised societies in pre-colonial Zambia: Bemba kingdom – located in northern Zambia ruled by the king known as chief Chitimukulu. The Lozi kingdom - located in western Zambia ruled by the Litunga. The Lunda kingdom – located in the Luapula region led by Chief Mwata Kazembe. The Chewa kingdom – located in the Eastern region led by Chief Gawa Undi. The Ngoni kingdom – located in Eastern Zambia led by Chief Mpezeni.

Features of centralised societies: In a centralised society, chiefs had centralised political power. The Chief was helped to rule by the senior chiefs, sub-chiefs and Village headmen. Chiefs controlled trade routes for salt, ivory, copper and slaves. All the centralised societies received tributes in form of food, labour and goods from their subjects and the conquered people. Chiefs also organised traditional ceremonies which brought all the people within the kingdom together. For example, Ukusefya pa Ngwena (Bemba), Kuomboka(Lozi), Ncwala(Ngoni), Umutomboko (Lunda), Likumbi Lya Mize(Luvale) Kulamba(Chewa). Centralised societies had well organised army to defend the kingdom and expand territories. For example, the Ngoni had a warrior based system.

Integrating features of centralised societies in modern Zambia:

Centralised political power: Zambia being a republic has an elected president who is helped to rule by ministers, members of parliament, councilors and district commissioners. This clearly shows how the Zambian system of governance functions similarly to chiefs and paramount chiefs. Zambian government has embraced traditional leadership through the House of Chiefs established in 1965 to date with 50 members. Chiefs have continued to be in charge of traditional affairs and offer advice the president on traditional matters.

Monopoly of trade: The government manages economies through trade regulations and public services.

Tribute system: The government also collects taxes from its citizens through the Zambia Revenue Authority and distributes to different sectors or areas of needy to foster development. This can be likened to the tribute system of pre-colonial Zambia.

Traditional ceremonies: In pre-colonial Zambia, traditional leaders used traditional ceremonies to unite people. Similarly in modern Zambia, successive governments have been using traditional ceremonies to foster national unity.

Organised military structure: Centralised societies had organised armies to maintain stability. Modern societies rely on defense forces and police for national security. Like the Chiefs and Kings in pre-colonial Zambia, the President of Zambia is the commander of the armed forces.

questions: In most of the Pre-colonial Centralised societies of Zambia, the Chief controlled the Army. Why is it an important aspect of every society including modern Zambia to have an army centrally controlled?

Most Pre-colonial societies in Zambia were located in specific regions of the country. However, people have now changed and different ethnic groups are now settling in any part of the country. What factors have contributed to this change in settlement patterns?
How can historical awareness contribute to fostering a sense of understanding, respect and unity among various ethnic and cultural groups within Zambia?
